text:

title: viz-1-aggression
text:
Clean the variables

brexit_data <- brexit_data %>%
  mutate(
    
    Brexit_Attitude = factor(
      Brexit_Attitude,
      levels = c("Brexiteer", "Remainer", "Unknown")
    ),
    
    Cross_Cutting_without_Counters = recode(
      Cross_Cutting_without_Counters,
      "Like_Minded" = "Like-minded",
      "Cross_Cutting" = "Cross-cutting",
      "Unknown" = "Unknown"
    ),
    
    Cross_Cutting_w_Counters = recode(
      Cross_Cutting_w_Counters,
      "Like_Minded" = "Like-minded",
      "Cross_Cutting" = "Cross-cutting",
      "Unknown" = "Unknown"
    ),
    
    Comment_Type = str_replace_all(Comment_Type, "_", " "),
    
    Chain_Comment = factor(
      Chain_Comment,
      levels = c(0,1),
      labels = c("Standalone","Chain")
    ))

table(brexit_data$Brexit_Attitude)


Brexiteer  Remainer   Unknown 
     3613       781       606 

table(brexit_data$Cross_Cutting_without_Counters)


Cross-cutting   Like-minded       Unknown 
         1097          3304           599 



Behavioral aggression by Brexit Identity

ggplot(
  brexit_data %>% filter(!is.na(Commenter_BA_Score)),
  aes(x = Commenter_BA_Score, fill = Brexit_Attitude)
) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Brexit_Attitude, ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Behavioral aggression differs across Brexit political identities",
    subtitle = "Higher scores indicate more aggressive or hostile comment language",
    x = "Behavioral aggression score",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Brexit identity"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









brexit_data %>%
  group_by(Brexit_Attitude) %>%
  summarise(
    n_comments = n(),
    mean_aggression = mean(Commenter_BA_Score, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_aggression = median(Commenter_BA_Score, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  Brexit_Attitude n_comments mean_aggression median_aggression
  <fct>                <int>           <dbl>             <dbl>
1 Brexiteer             3613           0.985                 1
2 Remainer               781          -0.970                -1
3 Unknown                606           0                     0


#Final version

agg_summary <- brexit_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(Commenter_BA_Score)) %>%
  group_by(Brexit_Attitude) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_aggression = mean(Commenter_BA_Score, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

ggplot(
  brexit_data %>% filter(!is.na(Commenter_BA_Score)),
  aes(x = Commenter_BA_Score, fill = Brexit_Attitude)
) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.55, color = NA) +
  geom_vline(
    data = agg_summary,
    aes(xintercept = mean_aggression),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~Brexit_Attitude, ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Behavioral aggression differs across Brexit political identities",
    subtitle = "Higher scores indicate more aggressive or hostile comment language; dashed lines show group means",
    x = "Behavioral aggression score",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Brexit identity"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









library(ggrepel)

comment_counts <- brexit_data %>%
  count(Commenter_ID, name = "n_comments") %>%
  arrange(desc(n_comments)) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number())

top_users <- comment_counts %>%
  slice_max(n_comments, n = 8) %>%
  mutate(label = paste("User", rank))

top_users <- comment_counts %>%
  slice_max(n_comments, n = 8) %>%
  mutate(label = paste("User", rank))

ggplot(comment_counts, aes(x = rank, y = n_comments)) +
  
  geom_col(fill = "grey80", width = 0.9) +
  
  geom_col(
    data = top_users,
    fill = "#C55644",
    width = 0.9
  ) +
  
  geom_text_repel(
    data = top_users,
    aes(label = label),
    size = 3.5,
    box.padding = 0.3
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "A small number of users dominate the Brexit comment discussion",
    subtitle = "Most participants comment once, while a few contribute repeatedly",
    x = "Commenter rank",
    y = "Number of comments"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )










why this version is better
It fixes several visual problems:

Highlights important information Instead of thousands of identical bars, it highlights the top commenters.
Makes the story clearer The title now tells the story: “A small number of users dominate the discussion.”
Labels extreme cases ggrepel prevents overlapping labels.

This visualization shows that participation in the Brexit discussion is highly concentrated. While thousands of users contributed at least one comment, most posted only once. A small minority of highly active participants contributed many more comments than the average user. I revised the draft visualization to highlight these extreme contributors while keeping the full distribution visible.

title: Progress Update 1
text:
Created a Quarto website project with a blog listing page and site pages (Home, About, Blog, Visualizations).
Rendered the site to the docs/ folder for GitHub Pages deployment.
Set up a GitHub repository for the final project and pushed the full site structure to main.
Resolved GitHub large file rejection by removing the large dataset from version control and adding a .gitignore to prevent future large data commits.
Created a reproducible project structure with data/, data_clean/, and scripts/ folders.
Added an initial Visualizations page and prepared it for publishing the first chart.
Next steps: clean a small dataset using a script, publish the first visualization with brief interpretation, and share the GitHub repo with a colleague for peer review.

title: Progress Update 1
text:
Created a Quarto website project with a blog listing page and site pages (Home, About, Blog, Visualizations).

Next steps: clean a small dataset using a script, publish the first visualization with brief interpretation, and share the GitHub repo with a colleague for peer review.

library(ggrepel)

brexit_data <- read_csv(
  here("data", "Brexit 5000 Sample Anonymized.csv")
)

New names:
Rows: 5000 Columns: 14
── Column specification
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr
(10): Commenter_ID, Comment_ID, Date, Brexit_Attitude, Commenter_Total_R... dbl
(4): ...1, Argument, Commenter_BA_Score, Chain_Comment
ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ
Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
• `` -> `...1`

brexit_data <- brexit_data %>%
  mutate(
    Brexit_Attitude = factor(
      Brexit_Attitude,
      levels = c("Brexiteer", "Remainer", "Unknown")
    ),
    Cross_Cutting_without_Counters = recode(
      Cross_Cutting_without_Counters,
      "Like_Minded" = "Like-minded",
      "Cross_Cutting" = "Cross-cutting",
      "Unknown" = "Unknown"
    )
  )

comment_counts <- brexit_data %>%
  count(Commenter_ID, name = "n_comments") %>%
  arrange(desc(n_comments)) %>%
  mutate(
    rank = row_number(),
    participant_label = paste("Participant", rank)
  )

n_users <- n_distinct(brexit_data$Commenter_ID)
pct_once <- mean(comment_counts$n_comments == 1)
pct_two_or_less <- mean(comment_counts$n_comments <= 2)

top10 <- comment_counts %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

top_users <- comment_counts %>%
  slice_max(n_comments, n = 8) %>%
  mutate(label = paste("P", rank))
## Why this matters

This visualization examines whether participation in Brexit comment discussions is broadly shared or dominated by a small number of highly active users. Participant IDs are anonymized and are **not shown** in the final graph.

title: viz-2 commenters
text:
Draft Version

ggplot(top10, aes(x = reorder(participant_label, n_comments), y = n_comments)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Draft: Top 10 commenters",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Number of comments"
  ) +
    theme_minimal()

title: viz-2 commenters
section: What was missing in the draft
text:
What was missing in the draft
The draft only showed the ten most active users. While readable, it did not show the full participation pattern, so it was harder to support the claim that a small number of participants dominate the discussion.

title: viz-2 commenters
text:
Final version

ggplot(comment_counts, aes(x = rank, y = n_comments)) +
  geom_col(fill = "grey85", width = 0.9) +
  geom_col(
    data = top_users,
    fill = "#D55E00",
    width = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = top_users,
    aes(label = label),
    size = 3.5,
    box.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = "gray50"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Participation in Brexit discussions is highly unequal",
    subtitle = "Most users commented once, while a small minority contributed repeatedly",
    x = "Commenter rank",
    y = "Number of comments"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray35"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: viz-2 commenters
text:
Interpretation
This shows that participation is highly concentrated: while many users appear in the discussion, a small minority account for repeated activity. I revised the original top-10 version to show the full ranked distribution so the inequality in participation is visible across the entire dataset.

title: Home
text:
This project analyzes a dataset of 5,000 anonymized comments related to Brexit discussions collected from social media platforms associated with three major campaign groups: LeaveEU, VoteLeave, and StrongerIn. The dataset captures how individuals interacted in online political discussions, including the tone of comments, the political orientation of participants, and whether discussions occurred between like-minded individuals or across opposing viewpoints.
Each row in the dataset represents one comment made by a participant in the discussion. The variables describe the commenter, the tone of the message, the political context of the discussion, and the interaction structure of the conversation.

title: Home
text:
This project analyzes a dataset of 5,000 anonymized comments related to Brexit discussions collected from social media platforms associated with three major campaign groups: LeaveEU, VoteLeave, and StrongerIn. The dataset captures how individuals interacted in online political discussions, including the tone of comments, the political orientation of participants, and whether discussions occurred between like-minded individuals or across opposing viewpoints.
Each row in the dataset represents one comment made by a participant in the discussion. The variables describe the commenter, the tone of the message, the political context of the discussion, and the interaction structure of the conversation.

title: Home
text:
Variable Descriptions
Commenter_ID
A unique anonymized identifier assigned to each participant in the discussion. It allows us to track how many comments each individual made without revealing personal identities.
Comment_ID
A unique identifier for each individual comment in the dataset.
Argument
The textual content of the comment. This field contains the actual message posted by the participant.
Date
The date on which the comment was posted.
Commenter_BA_Score
A behavioral aggression score assigned to the comment. This metric captures the tone of the comment on a scale where higher values indicate more aggressive or confrontational language, while lower values indicate less aggressive or more conciliatory language.
Brexit_Attitude
The political stance of the commenter in relation to Brexit.
Possible categories include:

Brexiteer – supportive of leaving the European Union
Remainer – supportive of remaining in the European Union
Unknown – stance could not be clearly determined from the comment

Commenter_Total_Reactions

The total number of reactions (such as likes, shares, or other engagement indicators) received by the comment. This variable provides a measure of how much attention or engagement a comment generated.
Campaign

The campaign group associated with the discussion thread where the comment was posted.
The dataset includes comments from three major campaign spaces:

LeaveEU
VoteLeave
StrongerIn
These represent major campaign organizations involved in the Brexit debate.

Comment_Order

The position of the comment within a discussion thread. Lower values indicate comments earlier in the conversation, while higher values indicate later responses.
Comment_Type

Describes the type of comment made in the discussion (for example, a direct argument, response, or other interaction type).
Cross_Cutting_w_Counters

Indicates whether the interaction involved disagreement between opposing viewpoints with explicit counterarguments.
Cross_Cutting_without_Counters

Indicates whether the interaction involved disagreement between opposing viewpoints without explicit counterarguments.
Chain_Comment

Indicates whether the comment is part of a longer conversational chain (a back-and-forth discussion) or a standalone comment.

Standalone – a single comment not part of a reply chain
Chain – a comment that is part of an ongoing discussion thread

title: Home
text:
Why These Variables Matter
Together, these variables allow us to explore several key questions about online political discourse during the Brexit debate:

Do people with different political identities communicate differently in terms of tone and aggression?
Do discussions mostly occur among like-minded participants, or do people engage with opposing viewpoints?
Are discussions dominated by a small number of highly active participants, or are they broadly distributed across many users?
Do more aggressive comments attract more engagement from other users?
These questions guide the visualizations presented in the project, which examine patterns of political identity, participation inequality, conversational structure, and engagement in online Brexit discussions.
